---
title: "GPX Analysis"
---

```{python}
from pathlib import Path
import json
import pandas as pd

manifest = json.loads(Path("data/routes.json").read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
routes = pd.DataFrame(manifest["routes"])
route_cards = routes[["title", "source"]].copy()
route_cards["distance_mi"] = routes["summary"].apply(lambda item: item["distance_mi"])
route_cards["elevation_loss_ft"] = routes["summary"].apply(lambda item: item["elevation_loss_ft"])
route_cards["segment_count"] = routes["summary"].apply(lambda item: item["segment_count"])
```

This project is now oriented around **Quarto documents and dashboards** rather than a custom browser app.
The Python package remains the analysis layer; Quarto is the publishing layer.

## Working Model

1. GPX inputs live in `gpx_data/`.
2. Python scripts export route summaries and GeoJSON into `quarto/data/`.
3. Quarto pages read those static artifacts and render route notes, maps, and summary dashboards.

## Current Curated Routes

```{python}
route_cards.rename(
    columns={
        "title": "Route",
        "source": "Source GPX",
        "distance_mi": "Miles",
        "elevation_loss_ft": "Descent (ft)",
        "segment_count": "Segments",
    }
)
```

## Local Authoring Loop

```powershell
$env:PYTHONPATH='.'
.\.venv\Scripts\python.exe scripts\build_quarto_data.py
quarto preview quarto
```

That keeps the data build separate from the document render, which is the right split for this repo.
